topic: grandkids visiting — teaching them to card wool

turns.content: the grandkids are here for the week and my house has not been this loud since lambing season. i love it but i am also exhausted and it is only tuesday
Ha — kids and lambs both know how to take over a house. How many grandkids are visiting, and how old are they?

three of them. two girls and a boy. the youngest is 6 and he has more energy than all my sheep combined. the girls are a little older, they can actually sit still for five minutes at a time. i am trying to teach them to card wool today
Carding wool — that's a great skill to pass along. Are they taking to it, or is it more of a battle to keep them focused?

the older girl gets it. she has good hands, you know. patient. she watched me do it a few times and then just started doing it herself. the other one keeps pulling too hard and making a mess. and the boy lasted about 90 seconds before he went outside to chase the dog

Sounds about right for the age range. The one with patient hands — that's a good sign. Carding takes a feel for the fiber that you can't really teach, you just have to develop it. Is she interested in the whole process, or just the carding part?

she wants to know everything. where does the wool come from, why do the sheep look different after shearing, what happens after carding. i told her after we card it we spin it and after we spin it we can weave with it. her eyes got big. she said can we do all of that this week. i said girl we are not building rome here, one step at a time

Ha — the ambition is great though. She wants to see the whole journey from sheep to something you can hold. That's how the best learners are. Maybe you could show her a finished piece so she can see where the process ends up, even if she's only learning the first step this week?

that is a good idea. i have some pieces around the house i can show her. let her feel the difference between raw wool and finished yarn and a woven piece. she needs to understand the wool changes at every step. it is not the same stuff by the time you are done with it

Exactly — letting her touch each stage makes it real in a way that just telling her about it doesn't. Raw fleece, carded rolag, spun yarn, finished weaving. Four completely different textures from the same sheep. That's a powerful thing for a kid to understand.

you know what else is funny. the boy came back inside covered in dirt and wanted to know if he could card the dog. i said no you cannot card the dog. the dog does not need carding. he said the dog is fluffy like the sheep. he is not wrong but still no

The logic is sound even if the application is wrong. Fluffy does not mean cardable — though I bet the dog disagreed. Sounds like he's having his own kind of adventure out there. Are the grandkids from around the area, or did they come from further away?

their mom — my daughter — lives in flagstaff now. so they are not out here all the time. that is why this week matters, you know. they need to touch the wool and smell the sheep and get dirt under their fingernails. they do not get that in town

That's exactly it. You're giving them something they can't get from a screen or a classroom — a physical connection to where things come from and how they're made. Those weeks at grandma's house stick with kids. They'll remember the feel of the wool and the smell of the sheep long after they forget what was on TV.
ahéhee'. okay i better go. i hear something crash in the kitchen which means the boy found something he should not have. send good thoughts for my survival this week

Good luck — sounds like you'll need the coffee supply doubled this week. Enjoy the chaos while it lasts. Those kids are lucky to have a grandmother who puts wool in their hands instead of a tablet. Go rescue your kitchen.
